translation.content: Since the release of his first RA Podcast in 2020, Tim Reaper has been grafting harder than ever, and the results speak for themselves. The Londoner has delivered a treasured batch of remixes for Special Request (one of which inspired 2024's biggest speed garage bootleg), kept the Simpsons-inspired hardcore project Globex Corp alive alongside longtime collaborator Dwarde and co-authored a critically acclaimed jungle LP on Hyperdub, the label's first. Add to that countless DJ sets and the sharp curation of his Future Retro outlet, and you get a sense of the scale of his contribution, not just to jungle, but to UK music at large. He stands as one of the most universally well-regarded of this current club generation for a reason. We knew we had to have him back. In the last year or so, signs of a shift have started to emerge. A release on Scratcha's DRMTK label and b2bs with cult DJs like Neffa-T and RIZ LA TEEF saw Tim Reaper move fluidly through footwork, UK funky and other club styles with the same confidence and energy he brings to jungle. One set in particular stands out: a session last December at Dalston dubstep party NLDC, during which he provoked a frenzied call for a reload via an obscure Shackleton tune. It was a moment that spoke volumes about his range. That same quality animates RA.1000: all seven and a half hours and 229 tracks of it. It begins with the grime-sampling weight of John T. Gast and ends with Stray & Halogenix's discombobulating drum & bass, all via hours of sleek US club, wobbly dubstep, modern techno and jungle of the atmospheric, headphone-friendly variety. Tim Reaper's output and ethos are so consistent and focused, delivering yet another nerdy showcase of hardcore, jungle and drum & bass would've surely been the easy play. But instead, he goes bolder and more vulnerable, venturing outside his comfort zone with the curiosity and integrity of fellow shapeshifters like dBridge, Calibre and ASC. It's not only another side of his artistry—thankfully for us, this exploratory mood feels like only the beginning. - Tom Gledhill How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? The idea behind this mix was to represent other styles of electronic music that I've been a bit self-conscious about openly expressing my interest in. I tried to approach selecting the tunes as if I wasn't Tim Reaper with my own motives to play certain tracks, whether they were by me or my mates, or forthcoming on my label, including the jungle section, which is less dance floor-oriented than I usually go for. In a world where technology is rapidly changing the way we DJ, how do you see the future of DJing and mixing evolving? I guess it'd allow DJs to transition more seamlessly through multiple genres in their sets, without there being limitations related to having to own multiple styles of music in physical format or being limited in the pitch range you can work within, due to the equipment available to them.
translation.blurb: UK jungle's leading light delivers a mammoth seven and a half hours of exceptional club music for RA.1000, spanning genres, tempos and eras.
